summary: Ronacher argues that Python 3’s string/bytes split makes WSGI significantly harder to implement than in Python 2, especially because web protocols are byte-oriented while frameworks need Unicode. He explains that WSGI lacks a configured encoding, so forcing Unicode at the spec level leads to awkward, lossy-looking conversions and more complex middleware. A “fake Unicode” Latin-1 strategy is emerging, but he is unhappy with the added complexity and with standard-library decisions that hardwire UTF-8 in places. He fears a spec that forces repeated encode/decode cycles just to compensate for bytes behavior and library constraints. He concludes by urging more people to experiment with Python 3 and the evolving WSGI spec so problems surface early and the language and tooling improve.
key_points: Python 3’s bytes are no longer string-like, making common WSGI byte-based operations lower-level and more cumbersome.
Web protocols are byte/ASCII-based, but WSGI has no encoding configuration, so Unicode at the interface creates ambiguity.
Standard library components introduced Unicode assumptions (latin1 headers, UTF-8 parsing) that break or limit real-world cases.
The draft WSGI-on-Python-3 spec uses a Latin-1 “fake Unicode” approach to preserve bytes, but it is unsatisfying in practice.
He proposes better bytes support and broader experimentation to avoid locking in a painful spec.
money_quotes: There are only unicode strings now and the bytestrings got replaced by things that are more like arrays than strings.
WSGI like HTTP or URIs are all based on ASCII or an encoding like latin1 or even different encodings.
There is no configuration for WSGI so the only thing we could do is forcing a specific charset for WSGI applications on Python 3.
It just feels like the more I play with it, the more unhappy I become with how the bytes object works and how the standard library behaves.
Right now I'm a little bit afraid we end up with a specification that requires use to do the encode/decode/encode/decode dance.
Because one thing is for certain: ASCII and bytes are here to stay.
The reason why Python 3 is not as good as it could be, is that far too few people look at it.
key_insight: WSGI on Python 3 risks becoming needlessly complex because byte-based web protocols clash with a Unicode-focused language/runtime and an under-tested, evolving spec.
